# Runbook: DDoS attack in progress

**Owner:** security-on-call  
**Severity:** SEV-1  
**Last updated:** 2026-05-08  
**Tested:** 2026-05-08 (DDoS simulation game-day)  

## Summary

This runbook covers response when the site is under a distributed denial-of-service attack. Symptoms include sudden traffic spikes from many source IPs, high CPU on edge servers, and degraded response times for legitimate users.

## Symptoms

Alertmanager page: DDoSDetected (paged when traffic exceeds 10x baseline for 3 min). Edge CPU at 100%. Legitimate user requests timing out. WAF logs show suspicious patterns.

## Diagnosis

1. Identify the attack pattern in the WAF logs (UDP flood, HTTP flood, slowloris, etc.). 2. Identify the source IP ranges (usually many diverse IPs, often from specific countries or ASNs). 3. Check if the attack targets a specific endpoint or the entire site.

## Mitigation

1. Enable the DDoS mitigation profile in the WAF (drops known-bad patterns). 2. Engage the upstream CDN/DDoS protection service (Cloudflare, AWS Shield, etc.) to absorb the attack at the edge. 3. Rate-limit aggressive clients at the edge. 4. If the attack targets a specific endpoint: temporarily restrict that endpoint to authenticated users. 5. Communicate status every 5 minutes per SEV-1 SLA. 6. Post a customer-facing status page update.

## Recovery

1. Verify edge CPU returns to normal. 2. Verify legitimate user requests succeed. 3. Keep DDoS mitigation enabled for 24 hours after the attack subsides (to prevent re-attack).

## Postmortem

For SEV-1 incidents, a blameless postmortem is required within 3 business days. Action items typically include: improve DDoS detection time, tune WAF rules based on the attack pattern, and review the upstream protection configuration.

## Escalation

SEV-1 incidents are immediately escalated to engineering leadership and customer success.

# Runbook: Search index corruption

**Owner:** search-on-call  
**Severity:** SEV-3  
**Last updated:** 2026-03-18  
**Tested:** 2026-03-18 (index rebuild game-day)  

## Summary

This runbook covers response when the search index is corrupted or returning inconsistent results. Symptoms include search results that do not match the underlying data, or search returning 500 errors for all queries.

## Symptoms

Alertmanager page: SearchIndexErrors (paged when error rate exceeds 5% for 5 min). Search results return inconsistent data. Customer reports of 'I cannot find X' or 'search returns wrong results'.

## Diagnosis

1. Check the search cluster health: curl <search-host>:9200/_cluster/health. 2. Check the index status: curl <search-host>:9200/_cat/indices?v. 3. Compare a sample search result with the underlying database to confirm corruption. 4. Check recent deploys or data migrations that may have left the index out of sync.

## Mitigation

1. If the index is corrupted: trigger a full reindex from the source database. 2. If the index is missing shards: restore from snapshot. 3. If the cluster is unhealthy: restart the affected nodes. 4. Communicate status to incidents channel per SEV-3 SLA.

## Recovery

1. Verify search results match the underlying database for a sample of queries. 2. Verify search error rate returns below 1%. 3. Verify index size and shard count are healthy.

## Postmortem

For SEV-3 incidents, a brief postmortem note is sufficient. Action items typically include: fix the root cause of corruption, add monitoring for index-data consistency, and document the reindex procedure.

## Escalation

If search is completely down: escalate to SEV-2.

# Design Doc: User notification system redesign

**Author:** @platform-team  
**Status:** Proposed  
**Reviewers:** @growth-team, @mobile-team, @infra-team  
**Last updated:** 2026-06-10

## Overview

This design proposes a unified user notification system that consolidates email, SMS, push, and in-app notifications into a single pipeline. Today, each channel is implemented as a separate service with duplicated logic for templating, throttling, and delivery tracking. The redesign reduces code duplication, improves delivery reliability, and enables cross-channel features (e.g., fall back from push to email if the user does not open the push within 30 minutes).

## Goals and Non-Goals

Goals: (1) Single pipeline for all notification channels. (2) Consistent templating across channels. (3) Cross-channel fallback rules. (4) Unified delivery tracking and analytics. Non-goals: (1) Marketing campaign management (separate system). (2) Transactional email deliverability optimization (separate workstream). (3) Real-time chat-style notifications (out of scope).

## Background

The current notification system was built channel-by-channel over 5 years. Email is handled by a service using SendGrid, SMS by a service using Twilio, push by a service using FCM/APNs, and in-app by the frontend. Each service has its own template format, its own throttling logic, and its own delivery tracking. This duplication has caused several bugs (e.g., throttling logic differs between channels, leading to inconsistent user experience). The Q3 platform roadmap calls for consolidation.

## Detailed Design

The new system is a single pipeline: (1) Producer publishes a Notification event to the notifications topic. (2) Router consumes the event, applies cross-channel fallback rules, and fans out to per-channel workers. (3) Per-channel workers handle channel-specific delivery (templating, throttling, delivery). (4) Delivery tracker records success/failure to the analytics store. The pipeline is implemented as a Kafka-based event stream with workers in the existing services platform. Templates use a unified Handlebars-based DSL that compiles to per-channel formats.

## Alternatives Considered

Option A: Consolidate into a single service. Pro: simpler architecture. Con: single point of failure, harder to scale per-channel. Option B: Keep separate services, extract shared logic into a library. Pro: less change. Con: still duplicated boilerplate, no cross-channel features. Option C (chosen): Event-driven pipeline with shared routing/fallback logic and per-channel workers. Pro: flexible, scales per-channel, enables cross-channel features. Con: more moving parts.

## Cross-Cutting Concerns

Observability: All pipeline stages emit structured logs and metrics. The delivery tracker provides per-user delivery history accessible via API. Security: Notification content may contain PII; the pipeline encrypts content at rest and redacts PII in logs. Cost: Per-channel cost tracking is built in (each channel worker reports cost per notification).

## Open Questions

1. How do we handle backward compatibility for existing template formats? 2. What is the migration plan for existing per-channel services? 3. How do we handle rate limits that differ per channel? 4. What is the rollback plan if the new pipeline has issues?

## Rollout Plan

Phase 1 (Q3): Build the pipeline skeleton, migrate email. Phase 2 (Q4): Migrate push and in-app. Phase 3 (Q1 next year): Migrate SMS, deprecate old services. Each phase includes a 2-week dual-write window and a 4-week dual-read shadowing window before cutover.

# Design Doc: Search infrastructure migration to vector search

**Author:** @search-team  
**Status:** Accepted  
**Reviewers:** @ml-team, @platform-team  
**Last updated:** 2026-05-20

## Overview

This design proposes migrating the product search from keyword-based (Elasticsearch) to vector-based semantic search. Vector search enables semantic similarity (e.g., searching for 'running shoes' returns results for 'jogging sneakers' even without keyword overlap). The migration is incremental: keyword search runs in parallel during the transition, and we gradually shift the blend toward vector results based on relevance metrics.

## Goals and Non-Goals

Goals: (1) Enable semantic similarity search. (2) Maintain or improve relevance vs keyword search. (3) Keep p99 latency under 200ms. (4) Support the existing query API with no breaking changes. Non-goals: (1) Image or video search (separate workstream). (2) Personalized ranking (existing system). (3) Cross-language search (future work).

## Background

Our current keyword search (Elasticsearch with BM25) has been the source of several relevance complaints from customers. The most common complaint: customers search for a concept but use different words than the product descriptions (e.g., searching 'wireless earbuds' returns nothing because products are tagged 'Bluetooth headphones'). Vector search, which embeds both query and product into a shared embedding space, handles this naturally. We have run an offline evaluation showing vector search improves relevance by 18% on our internal benchmark.

## Detailed Design

The new system uses a sentence-transformer model to embed queries and product descriptions into 384-dimensional vectors. At index time, we compute embeddings for all products and store them in a vector index (FAISS or pgvector). At query time, we embed the query and retrieve the top-K nearest neighbors. The final ranking blends vector results with keyword results (BM25) using a learned linear combination. The embedding model is served as a sidecar in the search service to keep latency low.

## Alternatives Considered

Option A: Pure vector search. Pro: best semantic recall. Con: loses exact-match capability, slower for high-precision queries. Option B: Pure keyword search (status quo). Con: doesn't address the relevance complaints. Option C (chosen): Hybrid (vector + keyword blend). Pro: best of both. Con: more complex, need to tune the blend.

## Cross-Cutting Concerns

Observability: Per-query metrics for vector vs keyword contribution to the final ranking. Model updates: The embedding model can be updated independently of the search index, with a shadow-evaluation period. Cold start: For new products, embeddings are computed at index time (no cold-start latency).

## Open Questions

1. Which embedding model to use? (We have evaluated 3 candidates.) 2. How to handle products without good text descriptions (e.g., image-only listings)? 3. How to handle query language detection for multi-language support?

## Rollout Plan

Phase 1 (Q3): Index embeddings for all products, serve in shadow mode (results not shown to users). Phase 2 (Q4): Enable for 10% of traffic, compare relevance metrics. Phase 3 (Q1): Ramp to 100% if metrics are positive.

# Design Doc: API rate limiting overhaul

**Author:** @api-platform  
**Status:** Accepted  
**Reviewers:** @security-team, @customer-success  
**Last updated:** 2026-04-15

## Overview

This design proposes overhauling the API rate limiting system to support per-tenant, per-endpoint, and per-tier limits with a unified configuration system. Today, limits are hardcoded in multiple places, making it hard to adjust for special customers or to respond to abuse. The overhaul centralizes limit configuration, makes it dynamically adjustable, and adds observability.

## Goals and Non-Goals

Goals: (1) Centralized limit configuration. (2) Per-tenant overrides. (3) Per-endpoint granularity. (4) Dynamic adjustment without redeploy. (5) Observability (limit hits, near-misses). Non-goals: (1) Per-user limits (defer to a future workstream). (2) DDoS protection (handled by the WAF/CDN).

## Background

The current rate limiting is implemented as a middleware in each API service, with limits hardcoded in config files. This has caused several issues: (1) When a customer legitimately needs higher limits, we must redeploy all services. (2) When we detect abuse, we cannot quickly lower limits for the abusive tenant. (3) We have no visibility into how close tenants are to their limits (we only see hits, not near-misses). The overhaul addresses all three.

## Detailed Design

The new system uses a central rate-limit service that all API services call. The service maintains a Redis-backed sliding-window counter per (tenant, endpoint) pair. Limits are stored in a config database (Postgres) and cached in Redis for fast lookup. The config supports per-tenant overrides (e.g., enterprise tenant X gets 10x the default limit on endpoint Y). The service emits metrics for limit hits and near-misses (configurable threshold, e.g., 80% of limit).

## Alternatives Considered

Option A: Keep per-service middleware, add a shared config file. Pro: less change. Con: still requires redeploy for limit changes, no central observability. Option B (chosen): Central rate-limit service. Pro: dynamic config, central observability, per-tenant overrides. Con: new service to operate, adds a network hop. Option C: Use a managed service (e.g., AWS API Gateway). Pro: no ops. Con: vendor lock-in, less flexible.

## Cross-Cutting Concerns

Observability: Metrics for limit hits, near-misses, and config changes. Security: The rate-limit service is internal-only and authenticated via mTLS. Cost: Redis memory scales with active (tenant, endpoint) pairs; we estimate 50MB for our current scale.

## Open Questions

1. What is the right default limit per endpoint? 2. How do we handle limit changes for active connections? 3. What is the fallback behavior if the rate-limit service is down? (Fail open or fail closed?)

## Rollout Plan

Phase 1 (Q3): Build the rate-limit service, migrate one endpoint as a pilot. Phase 2 (Q4): Migrate all public API endpoints. Phase 3 (Q1): Add per-tenant override UI for customer success.

# Design Doc: Event sourcing for the order pipeline

**Author:** @orders-team  
**Status:** Proposed  
**Reviewers:** @platform-team, @data-team  
**Last updated:** 2026-06-05

## Overview

This design proposes migrating the order pipeline from a CRUD model (current state in a relational database) to an event-sourced model (append-only event log, current state derived by replaying events). Event sourcing provides a complete audit trail, enables time-travel debugging, and simplifies certain types of analytics.

## Goals and Non-Goals

Goals: (1) Complete audit trail of all order state changes. (2) Time-travel debugging (reconstruct state at any point in time). (3) Simplified analytics (events are the source of truth). Non-goals: (1) Migrate other pipelines (separate workstreams). (2) Change the public API (the API should continue to return the current state, derived from events). (3) Real-time event streaming to external consumers (separate workstream).

## Background

The current order pipeline uses a CRUD model: orders are rows in a Postgres table, updated in place. This has several limitations: (1) No audit trail of state changes (we only see the current state, not the history). (2) Debugging is hard (we cannot reconstruct what the order looked like at a specific point in time). (3) Analytics queries require a separate ETL pipeline that snapshots the database periodically. Event sourcing addresses all three.

## Detailed Design

The new system stores all order state changes as events in an append-only log (Kafka). Each event has a type (OrderCreated, OrderPaid, OrderShipped, etc.), a timestamp, an order ID, and the relevant fields. The current state of an order is derived by replaying all events for that order. The replay is done on-demand (when the order is queried) and cached in a read-side projection (Postgres) for fast lookup. The API continues to return the same shape as before (the read-side projection handles the translation).

## Alternatives Considered

Option A: Add audit logging alongside CRUD. Pro: less change. Con: dual-write complexity, no time-travel. Option B (chosen): Full event sourcing. Pro: complete audit trail, time-travel, simplified analytics. Con: significant change, new infrastructure, team needs to learn the pattern. Option C: Use a managed event-sourcing platform (e.g., EventStoreDB). Pro: less infrastructure to build. Con: vendor lock-in.

## Cross-Cutting Concerns

Observability: The event log is the source of truth; all metrics are derived from it. Schema evolution: Events are versioned; consumers must handle multiple versions. Data retention: Events are retained for 7 years (compliance); snapshots are taken periodically to bound replay time.

## Open Questions

1. How do we handle event ordering across services? 2. What is the snapshot frequency to bound replay time? 3. How do we handle schema changes for events already in the log? 4. How do we handle GDPR right-to-be-forgotten (events are immutable)?

## Rollout Plan

Phase 1 (Q4): Build the event log infrastructure, dual-write for new orders. Phase 2 (Q1): Migrate read paths to use the event log projection. Phase 3 (Q2): Deprecate the old CRUD writes.

# Design Doc: Multi-region active-active deployment

**Author:** @infra-team  
**Status:** Proposed  
**Reviewers:** @platform-team, @data-team  
**Last updated:** 2026-06-12

## Overview

This design proposes migrating from active-passive multi-region (current) to active-active multi-region. In active-active, all regions serve traffic simultaneously; in active-passive, only one region serves traffic and the other is on standby. Active-active improves availability (no failover delay) and reduces latency for global users (they connect to the nearest region).

## Goals and Non-Goals

Goals: (1) Serve traffic from all regions simultaneously. (2) Sub-second failover (vs minutes in active-passive). (3) Lower latency for global users. (4) Higher overall availability (a regional outage degrades gracefully rather than causing a full failover). Non-goals: (1) Data residency compliance (active-active makes this harder; separate workstream). (2) Cost reduction (active-active may increase cost; accepted trade-off).

## Background

Our current deployment is active-passive: US-East serves traffic, US-West is on standby. If US-East fails, we manually failover to US-West (takes 5-15 minutes). This failover time has caused several customer-visible outages. Active-active eliminates the failover time entirely: both regions always serve traffic, and if one fails, the other simply absorbs more load. The trade-off is increased complexity (data consistency across regions, conflict resolution).

## Detailed Design

The new deployment runs identical stacks in both regions. Traffic is routed via global load balancing (anycast or geo-DNS) to the nearest healthy region. Data is replicated asynchronously between regions (with a small replication lag, typically under 1 second). Writes are accepted in both regions; conflicts are resolved using last-writer-wins for most fields, and application-specific logic for fields that require it (e.g., inventory counters). Read-after-write consistency is provided via session affinity (a user's writes are routed back to the same region for a short window).

## Alternatives Considered

Option A: Keep active-passive, improve failover automation. Pro: less change. Con: still has failover delay, still has downtime. Option B (chosen): Active-active. Pro: no failover delay, lower latency. Con: data consistency complexity, conflict resolution. Option C: Active-active with synchronous replication. Pro: stronger consistency. Con: high write latency, cross-region dependency.

## Cross-Cutting Concerns

Observability: Per-region metrics for traffic, latency, error rate. Data consistency: Conflict resolution logic is tested in CI. Cost: Roughly 2x the infrastructure cost (both regions always serve); accepted trade-off for availability.

## Open Questions

1. How do we handle cross-region data consistency for fields that cannot tolerate conflicts (e.g., financial transactions)? 2. What is the right conflict resolution policy for inventory counters? 3. How do we handle GDPR data residency requirements? 4. What is the rollback plan if active-active has issues?

## Rollout Plan

Phase 1 (Q4): Build the data replication infrastructure. Phase 2 (Q1): Dual-write to both regions. Phase 3 (Q2): Enable read traffic in both regions for internal users. Phase 4 (Q3): Enable for all users.

# Design Doc: Feature flag system overhaul

**Author:** @platform-team  
**Status:** Accepted  
**Reviewers:** @product-team, @security-team  
**Last updated:** 2026-05-28

## Overview

This design proposes overhauling the feature flag system to support gradual rollouts, per-user targeting, and experiment integration. Today, feature flags are simple boolean env vars; this limits our ability to do gradual rollouts or target specific user segments. The overhaul uses a dedicated feature flag service (self-hosted Unleash) with SDK integration in all client and server applications.

## Goals and Non-Goals

Goals: (1) Gradual rollouts (1%, 10%, 50%, 100%). (2) Per-user targeting (e.g., enable for enterprise customers first). (3) Integration with our experiment platform. (4) Audit trail for flag changes. Non-goals: (1) Remote config (separate workstream). (2) A/B test statistical analysis (existing experiment platform).

## Background

Our current feature flags are env vars set at deploy time. This has caused several issues: (1) We cannot do gradual rollouts (a flag is either on for everyone or off for everyone). (2) We cannot target specific user segments (e.g., enable for enterprise customers first). (3) We have no audit trail of who changed a flag and when. (4) Flag changes require a redeploy. The overhaul addresses all four.

## Detailed Design

The new system uses self-hosted Unleash as the flag service. All applications use the Unleash SDK to evaluate flags at request time. Flags are configured via the Unleash UI (or API). The UI supports gradual rollouts (percentage-based), per-user targeting (e.g., user attributes, segments), and scheduling (enable a flag at a specific time). All flag changes are logged in an audit trail. The SDK caches flag values locally to avoid a network hop on every evaluation.

## Alternatives Considered

Option A: Keep env-var flags, add a config service for dynamic values. Pro: less change. Con: no gradual rollouts, no per-user targeting. Option B (chosen): Full feature flag service. Pro: gradual rollouts, per-user targeting, audit trail. Con: new service to operate, SDK integration in all apps. Option C: Use a commercial feature flag service (LaunchDarkly). Pro: no ops. Con: expensive at our scale, vendor lock-in.

## Cross-Cutting Concerns

Observability: Flag evaluations are logged (with sampling) for analytics. Security: Flag configuration access is gated by role. Cost: Unleash is open-source; we self-host on existing Kubernetes.

## Open Questions

1. How do we handle flag dependencies (flag A requires flag B)? 2. What is the right default rollout strategy for new flags? 3. How do we handle flag cleanup (removing old flags)?

## Rollout Plan

Phase 1 (Q3): Deploy Unleash, migrate one team as a pilot. Phase 2 (Q4): Migrate all teams. Phase 3 (Q1): Deprecate env-var flags.

# Design Doc: Database connection pooling with PgBouncer

**Author:** @db-team  
**Status:** Accepted  
**Reviewers:** @platform-team  
**Last updated:** 2026-04-20

## Overview

This design proposes adding PgBouncer as a connection pooler in front of the Postgres database. Today, each application instance opens its own connections to Postgres, leading to connection count growth that has caused outages during traffic spikes. PgBouncer pools connections at the application level, reducing the total connection count to Postgres by 10x or more.

## Goals and Non-Goals

Goals: (1) Reduce Postgres connection count by 10x. (2) Improve resilience to traffic spikes. (3) Maintain or improve query latency. (4) No application code changes required. Non-goals: (1) Query result caching (separate workstream). (2) Read replica routing (PgBouncer supports this but is out of scope for this design).

## Background

Our current Postgres setup has a max connection limit of 500. With 20 application services each opening 25 connections, we hit the limit during traffic spikes. The connection exhaustion has caused several SEV-2 incidents. PgBouncer sits between the applications and Postgres, pooling connections so that 20 services with 25 connections each (500 total) become 20 services connecting to PgBouncer, which maintains a pool of 50 connections to Postgres. The 10x reduction eliminates the connection-exhaustion risk.

## Detailed Design

PgBouncer runs as a sidecar in each application's pod (or as a shared service, depending on the deployment pattern). Applications connect to PgBouncer using the standard Postgres protocol (no application code changes). PgBouncer maintains a pool of connections to Postgres and multiplexes application connections onto pool connections. We use transaction-level pooling (each transaction gets a pool connection, released when the transaction commits).

## Alternatives Considered

Option A: Increase Postgres max_connections. Pro: simple. Con: each connection uses memory; 1000 connections uses significantly more memory and can degrade performance. Option B (chosen): PgBouncer. Pro: reduces connection count, well-understood technology. Con: adds a network hop, some Postgres features (prepared statements, LISTEN/NOTIFY) don't work with transaction pooling. Option C: Use a connection pooler built into the application. Pro: no extra hop. Con: requires changes to every application.

## Cross-Cutting Concerns

Observability: PgBouncer metrics for pool utilization, wait time, and connection errors. Security: PgBouncer is internal-only and authenticated via mTLS. Compatibility: Applications using prepared statements must be configured to use unnamed statements (or we switch to session-level pooling, which has lower multiplexing efficiency).

## Open Questions

1. Transaction-level vs session-level pooling? (Transaction-level is more efficient but breaks some features.) 2. Per-pod sidecar or shared service? 3. How do we handle PgBouncer high availability?

## Rollout Plan

Phase 1 (Q3): Deploy PgBouncer as a pilot for one low-traffic service. Phase 2 (Q4): Migrate all services. Phase 3 (Q1): Tune pool sizes based on production metrics.

# Design Doc: Observability stack consolidation

**Author:** @observability-team  
**Status:** Proposed  
**Reviewers:** @platform-team, @security-team  
**Last updated:** 2026-06-01

## Overview

This design proposes consolidating our observability stack (metrics, logs, traces) into a unified Grafana-based platform. Today, we use 4 different backends (Prometheus for metrics, Loki for logs, Jaeger for traces, and a commercial APM for application performance). The consolidation reduces operational overhead, improves correlation across signals, and lowers cost.

## Goals and Non-Goals

Goals: (1) Single Grafana-based UI for all signals. (2) Unified alerting (one alerting pipeline for metrics, logs, traces). (3) Lower operational overhead. (4) Lower cost (consolidate vendors). Non-goals: (1) Replace the commercial APM for security/compliance use cases. (2) Build a custom observability backend (use Grafana stack as-is).

## Background

Our current observability stack has grown organically: Prometheus for metrics (self-hosted), Loki for logs (self-hosted), Jaeger for traces (self-hosted), and Datadog APM for application performance (commercial). The commercial APM is expensive ($200K/year) and overlaps significantly with what we could do in-house with OpenTelemetry + Grafana. The consolidation moves application performance monitoring to OpenTelemetry + Grafana, eliminating the commercial APM cost.

## Detailed Design

The new stack uses Grafana as the unified UI, with Prometheus (metrics), Loki (logs), and Tempo (traces, replacing Jaeger) as backends. All applications emit OpenTelemetry data (metrics, logs, traces) to an OpenTelemetry Collector, which fans out to the appropriate backend. Grafana correlates signals via trace IDs (a single request's metrics, logs, and traces are linked). Alerting uses Grafana Alerting (unified across signal types).

## Alternatives Considered

Option A: Keep current stack, optimize. Pro: no change. Con: still 4 backends, still high cost. Option B (chosen): Consolidate to Grafana stack. Pro: unified UI, lower cost, open-source. Con: migration effort, some APM features may be missing initially. Option C: Move fully to a commercial observability platform (Datadog, New Relic). Pro: less ops. Con: higher cost, vendor lock-in.

## Cross-Cutting Concerns

Observability: The stack is the observability platform; we dogfood it. Security: All telemetry data is internal-only and access-controlled. Cost: Estimated $50K/year savings by eliminating the commercial APM.

## Open Questions

1. Which OpenTelemetry Collector configuration to use? 2. How do we handle the transition for applications that currently use Datadog SDKs? 3. What is the retention policy for each signal type?

## Rollout Plan

Phase 1 (Q4): Deploy Grafana stack, migrate one application as a pilot. Phase 2 (Q1): Migrate all applications to OpenTelemetry. Phase 3 (Q2): Decommission Datadog APM.

# Design Doc: Secrets management with HashiCorp Vault

**Author:** @security-team  
**Status:** Accepted  
**Reviewers:** @platform-team, @infra-team  
**Last updated:** 2026-03-25

## Overview

This design proposes adopting HashiCorp Vault as the central secrets management system. Today, secrets (API keys, database passwords, TLS certificates) are stored in environment variables, config files, and a mix of secret stores. This is hard to audit, hard to rotate, and has been the source of several security incidents. Vault provides central storage, dynamic secrets, automatic rotation, and a complete audit trail.

## Goals and Non-Goals

Goals: (1) Central secrets storage. (2) Automatic rotation. (3) Complete audit trail. (4) Dynamic secrets where possible (e.g., short-lived database credentials). Non-goals: (1) Replace our existing IAM system (Vault integrates with it). (2) Manage customer-facing secrets (separate customer secrets system).

## Background

Our current secrets management is ad hoc: API keys in env vars, database passwords in config files, TLS certificates managed by cert-manager. This has caused issues: (1) No central audit trail (we cannot tell who accessed which secret when). (2) Rotation is manual and error-prone (a missed rotation has caused outages). (3) Secrets are often committed to git by mistake (we have had several near-misses caught in code review). Vault addresses all three.

## Detailed Design

Vault runs as a highly available cluster on our existing Kubernetes infrastructure. Applications authenticate to Vault using Kubernetes service account tokens (no shared secrets). Secrets are stored in Vault's KV store (for static secrets) or generated dynamically (for database credentials, which Vault can issue short-lived credentials for). Secrets are rotated automatically based on a configurable policy. All access is logged to an audit trail. The audit trail is shipped to our SIEM for security monitoring.

## Alternatives Considered

Option A: Keep current ad hoc approach, add more documentation. Pro: no change. Con: doesn't address the root cause. Option B (chosen): Vault. Pro: central, auditable, rotation, dynamic secrets. Con: new infrastructure, applications must be updated. Option C: Use a cloud-managed secret store (AWS Secrets Manager, GCP Secret Manager). Pro: less ops. Con: vendor lock-in, less flexible than Vault.

## Cross-Cutting Concerns

Observability: Vault metrics for access, rotation, and errors. Security: All access is audited and shipped to the SIEM. High availability: Vault runs in HA mode (3 or 5 nodes) with auto-unseal using cloud KMS.

## Open Questions

1. What is the right rotation policy for each secret type? 2. How do we handle application startup if Vault is unavailable? (Cache secrets locally with a short TTL.) 3. What is the migration plan for existing secrets?

## Rollout Plan

Phase 1 (Q3): Deploy Vault, migrate one team's secrets as a pilot. Phase 2 (Q4): Migrate all static secrets. Phase 3 (Q1): Enable dynamic database credentials.

# Design Doc: CI/CD pipeline standardization

**Author:** @platform-team  
**Status:** Accepted  
**Reviewers:** @all-engineering  
**Last updated:** 2026-04-30

## Overview

This design proposes standardizing CI/CD pipelines across all teams using a shared pipeline-as-code library. Today, each team maintains its own CI/CD configuration, leading to inconsistency (some teams have security scans, others do not), duplication (each team reimplements the same steps), and slow onboarding (new engineers must learn each team's pipeline). The standardization uses shared pipeline templates with team-specific overrides.

## Goals and Non-Goals

Goals: (1) Consistent CI/CD across all teams. (2) Shared security scans and quality gates. (3) Faster onboarding (new engineers use the standard pipeline). (4) Easier platform upgrades (change the template once, all teams benefit). Non-goals: (1) Replace the CI/CD platform (we use GitHub Actions; this design is about how we structure the pipelines within it). (2) Force all teams to use the exact same steps (teams can override).

## Background

Our current CI/CD setup is GitHub Actions with each team maintaining its own workflows. This has led to inconsistency: some teams run security scans, some do not; some teams have automated canary deploys, some do not; some teams have proper test coverage gates, some do not. The standardization introduces shared reusable workflows that each team calls, with the ability to override specific steps.

## Detailed Design

The standardization uses GitHub Actions reusable workflows. A central repo (aegisgate-ci) contains the reusable workflows: reusable-build.yml (build and test), reusable-scan.yml (security and quality scans), reusable-deploy.yml (deploy with canary). Each team's workflow file calls these reusable workflows, passing team-specific parameters (e.g., which service to deploy, which environment). Teams can add team-specific steps before or after the reusable workflows. The reusable workflows are versioned and updated centrally.

## Alternatives Considered

Option A: Document best practices, let teams adopt voluntarily. Pro: no enforcement. Con: slow adoption, inconsistent state. Option B (chosen): Shared reusable workflows. Pro: consistent, easy to upgrade. Con: requires teams to migrate. Option C: Adopt a CI/CD platform with built-in templates (e.g., CircleCI, Buildkite). Pro: less code to maintain. Con: vendor lock-in, migration cost.

## Cross-Cutting Concerns

Observability: Pipeline metrics (build time, success rate, deployment frequency) are tracked centrally. Security: Security scans are mandatory in the reusable workflows; teams cannot disable them. Documentation: The reusable workflows are documented with examples and a migration guide.

## Open Questions

1. How do we handle teams with unusual requirements (e.g., hardware-in-the-loop tests)? 2. What is the versioning policy for reusable workflows? 3. How do we handle breaking changes in reusable workflows?

## Rollout Plan

Phase 1 (Q3): Build the reusable workflows, migrate 3 pilot teams. Phase 2 (Q4): Migrate all teams. Phase 3 (Q1): Deprecate team-specific workflows that duplicate the reusable ones.

# Postmortem: INC-2026-042 — Checkout service outage due to payment processor rate limit

**Date of incident:** 2026-04-12  
**Duration:** 47 minutes  
**Severity:** SEV-2  
**Incident commander:** @payments-on-call  
**Status:** Resolved

## Summary

On April 12, 2026, the checkout service experienced a 47-minute outage where approximately 12% of checkout requests failed with HTTP 503. The root cause was an unannounced rate limit change by our payment processor that caused our request volume to exceed the new limit by 3x. Customer impact: approximately 3,200 failed checkouts, estimated $180K in lost revenue.

## Impact

Customer impact: 3,200 failed checkouts over 47 minutes. Revenue impact: approximately $180K in lost transactions (estimated based on average order value and conversion rate). Internal impact: 4 engineers paged, 1 incident commander, 1 customer success manager coordinating customer communications.

## Timeline (all times UTC)

14:23 - Payment processor changed rate limit from 1000 req/s to 350 req/s without notice.
14:25 - Checkout service started receiving 429 responses from payment processor.
14:27 - Alertmanager paged: PaymentProcessorRateLimited.
14:32 - Incident commander acknowledged, began investigation.
14:38 - Root cause identified (payment processor rate limit change).
14:42 - Failover payment processor enabled via feature flag.
14:55 - Checkout error rate returned to baseline.
15:10 - Status page updated with resolution.
15:10 - Incident closed.

## Root Cause

The payment processor changed our rate limit from 1000 req/s to 350 req/s without prior notice. Our checkout service was configured to retry on 429 responses, which amplified the load (retries + new requests exceeded the limit further). The retry amplification caused a cascading failure: the more we retried, the more we were rate-limited, the more we retried.

## Contributing Factors

1. No monitoring on payment processor rate limit headers (we did not detect the limit change until requests started failing).
2. Retry logic was aggressive (5 retries with exponential backoff, but the backoff was too short).
3. No automated failover to a secondary payment processor (failover required manual feature flag toggle).
4. Payment processor did not provide advance notice of rate limit changes (external factor, not directly actionable).

## What Went Well

1. Alertmanager detected the issue within 2 minutes of the rate limit change.
2. Incident commander was identified and took charge quickly.
3. Failover payment processor feature flag was already implemented (from a previous incident) and worked correctly.
4. Customer communications were clear and timely (status page updated within 30 minutes of detection).

## What Went Wrong

1. We were not monitoring the rate limit headers from the payment processor, so we did not detect the limit change proactively.
2. The retry logic amplified the problem instead of helping.
3. The failover required manual intervention; we should have automated it.
4. We did not have a pre-agreed communication plan with the payment processor for rate limit changes.

## Action Items

1. Add monitoring on payment processor rate limit headers (owner: @payments-on-call, due: 2026-04-26).
2. Implement exponential backoff with jitter in retry logic (owner: @payments-team, due: 2026-05-10).
3. Automate failover to secondary payment processor when rate limit headers indicate throttling (owner: @platform-team, due: 2026-05-24).
4. Establish a communication plan with payment processor for rate limit changes (owner: @payments-on-call, due: 2026-05-17).

# Postmortem: INC-2026-038 — Search results stale after index corruption

**Date of incident:** 2026-03-28  
**Duration:** 2 hours 15 minutes  
**Severity:** SEV-3  
**Incident commander:** @search-on-call  
**Status:** Resolved

## Summary

On March 28, 2026, the product search returned stale results for approximately 2 hours. The root cause was a corrupted search index caused by a failed index rotation that left the index in an inconsistent state. Customer impact: customers saw outdated product information (old prices, out-of-stock items shown as available), leading to confusion and some support tickets.

## Impact

Customer impact: approximately 15,000 search queries returned stale results over 2 hours 15 minutes. Support impact: 23 customer support tickets about 'search shows wrong information'. Revenue impact: minimal (search is informational; purchases use a separate product detail page that was not affected).

## Timeline (all times UTC)

09:15 - Scheduled index rotation began.
09:18 - Rotation failed midway due to a disk space issue on the search cluster.
09:20 - Alertmanager paged: SearchIndexRotationFailed.
09:25 - On-call investigated, found the index in an inconsistent state (partially rotated).
09:35 - Decision made to rebuild the index from scratch (rather than try to repair).
09:40 - Index rebuild started (estimated 90 minutes).
11:10 - Index rebuild completed.
11:25 - Search results verified against source database.
11:30 - Incident closed.

## Root Cause

A scheduled index rotation failed midway due to a disk space issue on the search cluster. The failure left the index in a partially-rotated state: some shards had new data, others had old data. The search service, when querying the index, received inconsistent results depending on which shard responded.

## Contributing Factors

1. The index rotation did not have a rollback mechanism (if it failed midway, we were left in an inconsistent state).
2. The search cluster did not have automated disk space monitoring (we did not detect the disk filling until the rotation failed).
3. The search service did not have a health check that detected inconsistent results across shards (it would have detected the issue earlier).

## What Went Well

1. The index rebuild procedure was well-documented and worked as expected.
2. The on-call engineer quickly identified the root cause and made the correct decision (rebuild vs repair).
3. The search service continued to serve results (even if stale) during the incident, rather than failing completely.

## What Went Wrong

1. The index rotation did not have a rollback mechanism.
2. We did not have disk space monitoring on the search cluster.
3. The search service did not have a health check for cross-shard consistency.
4. The incident took 2 hours to resolve; we should have been able to detect and fix this faster.

## Action Items

1. Add rollback mechanism to index rotation (owner: @search-team, due: 2026-04-15).
2. Add disk space monitoring to search cluster (owner: @infra-team, due: 2026-04-08).
3. Add cross-shard consistency health check to search service (owner: @search-team, due: 2026-04-22).
4. Investigate faster recovery options (e.g., serve from the last-known-good snapshot during rebuild) (owner: @search-team, due: 2026-05-06).

# Postmortem: INC-2026-051 — Authentication latency spike due to slow database query

**Date of incident:** 2026-05-15  
**Duration:** 1 hour 30 minutes  
**Severity:** SEV-3  
**Incident commander:** @auth-on-call  
**Status:** Resolved

## Summary

On May 15, 2026, the authentication service experienced elevated latency (p99 increased from 200ms to 4.5s) for approximately 1.5 hours. The root cause was a slow query introduced by a recent migration that added a new index but did not update the query planner statistics. Customer impact: users experienced slow login and slow page loads, but no authentication failures.

## Impact

Customer impact: elevated latency on all authenticated pages for 1.5 hours. No authentication failures. Support impact: 8 customer support tickets about 'the site is slow'. Revenue impact: minimal (no failed transactions, just slower experience).

## Timeline (all times UTC)

14:00 - New database migration deployed (added index on users.last_login_at).
14:15 - Auth service p99 latency started climbing.
14:30 - Alertmanager paged: AuthLatencyHigh.
14:35 - On-call investigated, found slow query in pg_stat_activity.
14:50 - Identified that the query planner was not using the new index (statistics were stale).
15:05 - Ran ANALYZE on the users table to update statistics.
15:15 - Auth service p99 latency returned to baseline.
15:30 - Incident closed.

## Root Cause

A recent database migration added a new index on users.last_login_at but did not update the query planner statistics. The query planner, unaware of the new index, continued to use a sequential scan. When the query was run frequently (on every authenticated request), the sequential scan caused the latency spike.

## Contributing Factors

1. The migration script did not include ANALYZE after creating the index.
2. We do not have automated detection of slow queries after migrations (we detected it via latency alerting, but a query-level alert would have been faster).
3. The auth service does not have query-level timeouts (a slow query could run indefinitely).

## What Went Well

1. The latency alerting detected the issue within 15 minutes.
2. The on-call engineer quickly identified the slow query in pg_stat_activity.
3. The fix (ANALYZE) was simple and effective.
4. No authentication failures occurred (just elevated latency).

## What Went Wrong

1. The migration script did not include ANALYZE.
2. We did not have query-level alerting.
3. The auth service does not have query-level timeouts.

## Action Items

1. Update all migration scripts to include ANALYZE after index creation (owner: @db-team, due: 2026-05-22).
2. Add query-level alerting for queries exceeding 1s (owner: @db-team, due: 2026-06-05).
3. Add query-level timeouts to the auth service (owner: @auth-team, due: 2026-06-12).
4. Add a post-migration performance check to the deployment pipeline (owner: @platform-team, due: 2026-06-19).

# Postmortem: INC-2026-029 — CDN cache purge accidentally triggered

**Date of incident:** 2026-03-10  
**Duration:** 35 minutes  
**Severity:** SEV-3  
**Incident commander:** @edge-on-call  
**Status:** Resolved

## Summary

On March 10, 2026, a mass CDN cache purge was accidentally triggered by a misconfigured deployment script, causing cache miss rate to spike from 5% to 85%. The origin servers were overwhelmed for approximately 35 minutes until the cache warmed back up. Customer impact: slow page loads for static assets, occasional 504 errors from the origin.

## Impact

Customer impact: slow page loads for 35 minutes, occasional 504 errors. Support impact: 12 customer support tickets about 'the site is slow' or 'images not loading'. Revenue impact: minimal (no failed transactions, just slower experience).

## Timeline (all times UTC)

11:20 - Deployment script ran with a misconfigured cache-purge flag.
11:21 - Mass cache purge triggered (all URLs).
11:22 - Cache miss rate spiked to 85%.
11:23 - Alertmanager paged: CDNCacheMissHigh.
11:25 - On-call investigated, found the accidental purge in the CDN logs.
11:30 - Decision made to warm the cache manually with the top 1000 URLs.
11:40 - Cache warming script started.
11:55 - Cache miss rate returned below 20%.
11:55 - Incident closed.

## Root Cause

A deployment script had a misconfigured cache-purge flag. The flag was intended to purge only the URLs affected by the deployment, but due to a configuration error, it purged all URLs. The misconfiguration was not caught in code review because the cache-purge flag was not treated as a sensitive parameter.

## Contributing Factors

1. The deployment script had access to a mass-purge capability (should have been restricted to specific URLs only).
2. The cache-purge operation was not gated by a confirmation step (it executed immediately).
3. We did not have automated testing for the deployment script's side effects.
4. The cache-warming procedure was manual and took time to execute.

## What Went Well

1. The latency alerting detected the issue within 1 minute.
2. The on-call engineer quickly identified the accidental purge.
3. The cache-warming procedure worked and recovered quickly.
4. No data loss occurred (the cache is a performance optimization, not a source of truth).

## What Went Wrong

1. The deployment script had access to a mass-purge capability.
2. The cache-purge operation was not gated by a confirmation step.
3. The cache-warming procedure was manual.
4. The misconfiguration was not caught in code review.

## Action Items

1. Restrict the deployment script's cache-purge capability to specific URLs only (owner: @platform-team, due: 2026-03-24).
2. Add a confirmation step to all cache-purge operations (owner: @edge-team, due: 2026-03-17).
3. Automate the cache-warming procedure (owner: @edge-team, due: 2026-04-07).
4. Add automated testing for deployment script side effects (owner: @platform-team, due: 2026-04-21).

# Postmortem: INC-2026-063 — Database failover caused brief connection drops

**Date of incident:** 2026-05-22  
**Duration:** 8 minutes  
**Severity:** SEV-2  
**Incident commander:** @db-on-call  
**Status:** Resolved

## Summary

On May 22, 2026, the primary database failed over to the replica due to a hardware issue on the primary host. The failover took 8 minutes, during which some database connections were dropped, causing request failures. Customer impact: approximately 2% of requests failed with database connection errors during the failover window.

## Impact

Customer impact: approximately 2% of requests failed with database connection errors for 8 minutes. Support impact: 18 customer support tickets about 'errors on the site'. Revenue impact: estimated $25K in lost transactions.

## Timeline (all times UTC)

03:14 - Primary database host experienced hardware issue (disk controller failure).
03:14 - Database connections started failing.
03:15 - Alertmanager paged: DatabaseUnreachable.
03:16 - On-call investigated, identified the hardware issue.
03:18 - Failover to replica initiated.
03:22 - Failover completed, replica promoted to primary.
03:22 - Database connections restored.
03:30 - All systems verified healthy.
03:30 - Incident closed.

## Root Cause

A hardware failure on the primary database host (disk controller failure) caused the database to become unreachable. The automatic failover to the replica was initiated but took 8 minutes to complete (longer than expected). During the failover window, some database connections were dropped, causing request failures.

## Contributing Factors

1. The failover took longer than expected (8 minutes vs the 2-minute SLO).
2. The application services did not handle connection drops gracefully (some retried with the same dead connection).
3. We did not have a connection-pool health check that would have detected and removed dead connections faster.

## What Went Well

1. The automatic failover was initiated (manual failover would have taken longer).
2. The on-call engineer quickly identified the hardware issue.
3. No data loss occurred (replica was up-to-date within 1 second).
4. The incident was resolved within the SLO for a hardware failure.

## What Went Wrong

1. The failover took longer than expected.
2. Application services did not handle connection drops gracefully.
3. We did not have a connection-pool health check.

## Action Items

1. Investigate why the failover took 8 minutes (owner: @db-team, due: 2026-06-05).
2. Add connection-drop handling to application services (owner: @platform-team, due: 2026-06-12).
3. Add connection-pool health checks to PgBouncer (owner: @db-team, due: 2026-06-19).
4. Consider synchronous replication for faster failover (owner: @db-team, due: 2026-07-03).

# Postmortem: INC-2026-018 — Worker OOMKilled due to memory leak in third-party library

**Date of incident:** 2026-02-18  
**Duration:** 2 hours  
**Severity:** SEV-3  
**Incident commander:** @workers-on-call  
**Status:** Resolved

## Summary

On February 18, 2026, background workers processing image uploads experienced OOMKills due to a memory leak in a third-party image processing library. The workers would run for approximately 4 hours before being OOMKilled, requiring manual restarts. Customer impact: image uploads were delayed by up to 30 minutes during the incident window.

## Impact

Customer impact: image uploads delayed by up to 30 minutes for 2 hours. Support impact: 6 customer support tickets about 'image upload not working'. Revenue impact: minimal (image uploads are not revenue-critical).

## Timeline (all times UTC)

08:00 - Workers started processing image uploads (scheduled batch job).
12:00 - Workers OOMKilled (first OOMKill event).
12:01 - Kubernetes restarted workers automatically.
12:05 - Alertmanager paged: WorkerOOMKilled.
12:10 - On-call investigated, found memory usage climbing steadily.
12:30 - Identified the third-party library as the source of the leak.
12:45 - Decision made to add a periodic restart as a workaround.
13:00 - Workaround deployed (workers restart every 2 hours).
13:00 - Image upload processing resumed normally.
14:00 - Incident closed (workaround in place, root cause fix tracked separately).

## Root Cause

A memory leak in a third-party image processing library caused worker memory usage to grow linearly with the number of images processed. After approximately 4 hours of processing, memory usage exceeded the worker limit and the worker was OOMKilled.

## Contributing Factors

1. The third-party library had a known memory leak (documented in their issue tracker, but we had not upgraded).
2. We did not have memory-usage alerting at a lower threshold (we only alerted at 80%, which was too late).
3. The workers did not have a periodic restart as a safety mechanism.

## What Went Well

1. Kubernetes automatically restarted the OOMKilled workers.
2. The on-call engineer quickly identified the third-party library as the source.
3. The workaround (periodic restart) was deployed quickly.
4. No data loss occurred (the image processing is idempotent).

## What Went Wrong

1. We had not upgraded the third-party library despite a known memory leak fix.
2. We did not have memory-usage alerting at a lower threshold.
3. The workers did not have a periodic restart as a safety mechanism.

## Action Items

1. Upgrade the third-party image processing library (owner: @workers-team, due: 2026-03-04).
2. Add memory-usage alerting at 50% threshold (owner: @platform-team, due: 2026-03-11).
3. Add periodic restart to all long-running workers (owner: @workers-team, due: 2026-03-18).
4. Establish a process for tracking third-party library updates (owner: @platform-team, due: 2026-04-01).

# Postmortem: INC-2026-055 — Kubernetes cluster autoscaler misconfiguration

**Date of incident:** 2026-05-29  
**Duration:** 45 minutes  
**Severity:** SEV-3  
**Incident commander:** @infra-on-call  
**Status:** Resolved

## Summary

On May 29, 2026, the Kubernetes cluster autoscaler was misconfigured after a routine config update, causing it to fail to scale up during a traffic spike. Pods were stuck in Pending state for 45 minutes until the misconfiguration was identified and fixed. Customer impact: slow response times and occasional 503 errors during the traffic spike.

## Impact

Customer impact: slow response times and occasional 503 errors for 45 minutes. Support impact: 14 customer support tickets about 'the site is slow' or 'errors'. Revenue impact: estimated $15K in lost transactions.

## Timeline (all times UTC)

16:00 - Routine config update to cluster autoscaler.
16:05 - Traffic spike began (marketing campaign launch).
16:06 - Pods started pending (cluster could not scale up).
16:08 - Alertmanager paged: PodsPending.
16:12 - On-call investigated, found the autoscaler not scaling.
16:25 - Identified the misconfiguration in the autoscaler config.
16:35 - Config rolled back.
16:40 - Cluster scaled up, pods scheduled.
16:45 - All systems verified healthy.
16:45 - Incident closed.

## Root Cause

A routine config update to the cluster autoscaler introduced a misconfiguration that prevented it from scaling up. The misconfiguration was not caught in the config review process because the review focused on syntax, not on the scaling behavior.

## Contributing Factors

1. The config review process focused on syntax, not behavior.
2. We did not have automated testing for autoscaler config changes.
3. The traffic spike was unexpected (marketing campaign launch was not coordinated with infra).

## What Went Well

1. The alert detected the issue within 2 minutes.
2. The on-call engineer quickly identified the misconfiguration.
3. The rollback procedure worked smoothly.
4. The cluster scaled up quickly after the fix.

## What Went Wrong

1. The config review process missed the behavioral issue.
2. We did not have automated testing for autoscaler config changes.
3. The marketing campaign launch was not coordinated with infra.

## Action Items

1. Add behavioral testing to the autoscaler config review process (owner: @infra-team, due: 2026-06-12).
2. Automate testing for autoscaler config changes in CI (owner: @infra-team, due: 2026-06-19).
3. Establish a process for coordinating marketing campaigns with infra (owner: @product-team, due: 2026-06-26).
4. Add a pre-deployment canary for autoscaler config changes (owner: @infra-team, due: 2026-07-03).

# Postmortem: INC-2026-071 — TLS certificate expired on internal service

**Date of incident:** 2026-06-08  
**Duration:** 1 hour 20 minutes  
**Severity:** SEV-3  
**Incident commander:** @security-on-call  
**Status:** Resolved

## Summary

On June 8, 2026, a TLS certificate on an internal service expired because the cert-manager renewal had been failing silently for 3 weeks. The expired certificate caused all mTLS connections to the service to fail, leading to cascading failures in dependent services. Customer impact: intermittent errors on features that depended on the internal service.

## Impact

Customer impact: intermittent errors on features that depended on the internal service for 1 hour 20 minutes. Support impact: 9 customer support tickets about 'feature X not working'. Revenue impact: minimal (the affected features were not revenue-critical).

## Timeline (all times UTC)

10:00 - TLS certificate on internal service expired (cert-manager renewal had been failing silently).
10:01 - mTLS connections to the service started failing.
10:02 - Dependent services started returning errors.
10:05 - Alertmanager paged: InternalServiceUnavailable.
10:15 - On-call investigated, found the certificate expiration.
10:30 - Manually issued and deployed a new certificate.
11:20 - All services verified healthy.
11:20 - Incident closed.

## Root Cause

The cert-manager renewal for an internal service certificate had been failing silently for 3 weeks due to a DNS challenge configuration issue. The cert-manager logs showed the failure, but the alert for cert-manager renewal failures was not configured for internal certificates (only for public-facing certificates).

## Contributing Factors

1. The cert-manager renewal failure alerting was not configured for internal certificates.
2. The DNS challenge configuration issue was not detected earlier.
3. We did not have automated certificate age monitoring for internal certificates.

## What Went Well

1. The alert detected the cascading failures within 3 minutes.
2. The on-call engineer quickly identified the certificate expiration as the root cause.
3. The manual certificate issuance procedure worked.
4. The fix was straightforward (fix the DNS challenge config).

## What Went Wrong

1. The cert-manager renewal failure alerting was not configured for internal certificates.
2. The DNS challenge configuration issue went undetected for 3 weeks.
3. We did not have automated certificate age monitoring for internal certificates.

## Action Items

1. Configure cert-manager renewal failure alerting for all certificates (owner: @security-team, due: 2026-06-15).
2. Fix the DNS challenge configuration issue (owner: @security-team, due: 2026-06-15).
3. Add automated certificate age monitoring for all certificates (owner: @security-team, due: 2026-06-22).
4. Establish a quarterly audit of all internal certificates (owner: @security-team, due: 2026-07-01).

WHEREAS, Party A desires to engage Party B to provide certain professional services as more particularly described herein; WHEREAS, Party B represents that it has the requisite expertise, experience, and capacity to perform such services in a professional and workmanlike manner; and WHEREAS, the Parties desire to set forth their respective rights and obligations in writing.

NOW, THEREFORE, in consideration of the mutual promises and covenants contained herein, and for other good and valuable consideration, the receipt and sufficiency of which are hereby acknowledged, the Parties agree as follows:

1. SCOPE OF SERVICES. Party B shall perform the services described in Exhibit A (the "Services") in accordance with the terms of this Agreement and all applicable laws, regulations, and professional standards. Any material change to the scope of Services shall require a written amendment signed by an authorized representative of each Party.

2. COMPENSATION. As full and complete compensation for the performance of the Services, Party A shall pay Party B the fees set forth in Exhibit B. Invoices shall be submitted monthly and shall be payable within thirty (30) days of receipt. Disputed amounts may be withheld in good faith pending resolution of the dispute in accordance with Section 12 (Dispute Resolution).

3. TERM AND TERMINATION. This Agreement shall commence on the Effective Date and shall continue until the earlier of (a) completion of the Services, (b) termination as provided herein, or (c) the first anniversary of the Effective Date. Either Party may terminate this Agreement for cause upon thirty (30) days' written notice to the other Party specifying the cause in reasonable detail.

4. CONFIDENTIALITY. Each Party acknowledges that it may receive Confidential Information of the other Party. The receiving Party shall (a) hold the Confidential Information in strict confidence, (b) use the Confidential Information solely for purposes of performing its obligations hereunder, and (c) not disclose any Confidential Information to any third party without the prior written consent of the disclosing Party.

5. INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY. All deliverables produced by Party B in the performance of the Services (the "Deliverables") shall be the exclusive property of Party A upon payment in full of all fees due hereunder. Party B retains all rights to any pre-existing intellectual property incorporated into the Deliverables and grants Party A a non-exclusive, perpetual, royalty-free license to use such pre-existing intellectual property in connection with the Deliverables.

6. REPRESENTATIONS AND WARRANTIES. Party B represents and warrants that (a) it has the full corporate power and authority to enter into and perform this Agreement, (b) the execution and performance of this Agreement will not violate any other agreement to which it is a party, and (c) the Services will be performed in a professional and workmanlike manner in accordance with industry standards.

7. INDEMNIFICATION. Party B shall indemnify, defend, and hold harmless Party A and its officers, directors, employees, and agents from and against any and all claims, damages, losses, and expenses (including reasonable attorneys' fees) arising out of or relating to any breach of Party B's representations, warranties, or obligations under this Agreement.

8. LIMITATION OF LIABILITY. EXCEPT FOR BREACHES OF SECTION 4 (CONFIDENTIALITY) OR A PARTY'S INDEMNIFICATION OBLIGATIONS, IN NO EVENT SHALL EITHER PARTY BE LIABLE FOR ANY INDIRECT, INCIDENTAL, CONSEQUENTIAL, SPECIAL, OR PUNITIVE DAMAGES OF ANY KIND, REGARDLESS OF THE FORM OF ACTION, WHETHER IN CONTRACT, TORT (INCLUDING NEGLIGENCE), STRICT LIABILITY, OR OTHERWISE, EVEN IF SUCH PARTY HAS BEEN ADVISED OF THE POSSIBILITY OF SUCH DAMAGES.

9. GOVERNING LAW AND VENUE. This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware, without giving effect to any choice of law or conflict of law provisions. The Parties agree that any action arising out of or relating to this Agreement shall be brought exclusively in the state or federal courts located in Wilmington, Delaware, and each Party hereby submits to the personal jurisdiction of such courts.

10. ENTIRE AGREEMENT. This Agreement, together with all Exhibits attached hereto, constitutes the entire agreement of the Parties with respect to the subject matter hereof and supersedes all prior and contemporaneous agreements, understandings, negotiations, and discussions, whether oral or written, between the Parties.

11. AMENDMENT. No amendment, modification, or waiver of any provision of this Agreement shall be effective unless in writing and signed by an authorized representative of each Party. The failure of either Party to enforce any provision of this Agreement shall not constitute a waiver of such provision or of any other provision hereof.

12. DISPUTE RESOLUTION. The Parties shall attempt to resolve any dispute arising out of or relating to this Agreement through good-faith negotiation between senior representatives of the Parties. If the dispute is not resolved within thirty (30) days of initiation of negotiations, either Party may submit the dispute to mediation administered by JAMS pursuant to its then-current mediation rules. The mediation shall take place in Wilmington, Delaware, and the costs of mediation shall be shared equally by the Parties.

13. NOTICES. All notices, requests, consents, and other communications required or permitted under this Agreement shall be in writing and shall be deemed to have been duly given when (a) delivered personally, (b) sent by confirmed email, or (c) sent by certified or registered mail, return receipt requested, postage prepaid, in each case addressed as set forth on the signature page hereto or to such other address as a Party may designate by written notice given in accordance with this Section.

14. SEVERABILITY. If any provision of this Agreement is held to be invalid, illegal, or unenforceable in any respect under any applicable law in any jurisdiction, the remaining provisions of this Agreement shall continue in full force and effect; provided, however, that the Parties hereto shall negotiate in good faith to attempt to replace any invalid, illegal, or unenforceable provision with a valid, legal, and enforceable provision the economic effect of which most nearly approximates that of the invalid, illegal, or unenforceable provision.

15. ASSIGNMENT. Neither Party may assign this Agreement or any of its rights or obligations hereunder without the prior written consent of the other Party, which consent shall not be unreasonably withheld; provided, however, that either Party may assign this Agreement, without consent, to a successor in connection with a merger, acquisition, or sale of all or substantially all of its assets.
